question: How to compost tea?
title: How to Make Compost Tea: Scotty's Step-by-Step Guide
metaDescription: Compost tea puts billions of soil microbes directly on your plants and into your garden beds. Here's exactly how to make it, what it does, and why it works.
content:
I started making compost tea because I read about it in a book. Once I understood what was actually happening in that bucket, I couldn't stop thinking about it. You're basically brewing a living culture — taking a small amount of your best finished compost and multiplying the microbial life in it exponentially over 24 hours. Then you pour that life directly onto your soil.

That's not a metaphor. That's literally what's happening. And once you see it that way, the process stops feeling like a gardening trick and starts feeling like what it actually is: biology being put to work.

Here's how to do it right.

---

## What Compost Tea Actually Is

Compost tea — specifically aerobically activated compost tea, or AACT — is made by suspending a scoop of good finished compost in water, then aerating that water for 24 hours with an air pump. The aeration does two things: it keeps oxygen flowing so the beneficial aerobic microorganisms thrive and multiply, and it keeps the compost particles suspended so the microbial populations can colonize the liquid.

You start with a relatively small number of bacteria, fungi, protozoa, and nematodes from your compost. Over 24 hours of aeration with a small amount of food added — usually unsulfured molasses or kelp — those populations explode. You end up with a liquid teeming with billions of active microorganisms ready to colonize your soil or be absorbed through your plant's stomata as a foliar spray.

The University of Arizona Cooperative Extension describes the process as a way to take the biological diversity of good compost and extend its reach across a garden without needing to apply compost directly everywhere. The University of Connecticut's Soil Nutrient Analysis Laboratory has also documented the microbial dynamics involved.

Now, I want to be honest with y'all: university extension services are a little mixed on the research. Some studies show dramatic results, some show modest ones. What I can tell you from years of observation is that compost tea applied to biologically depleted soils produces visible, real results. On already-healthy living soil, the effects are more subtle. The degree of benefit depends entirely on the quality of your starting compost.

Garbage in, garbage out. Living biology in, living biology out.

## What You Need

This is a simple process. Here's your gear:

- A clean five-gallon bucket. I mean clean — rinse it thoroughly. Bad microbes can mess up a batch and produce something you don't want on your plants. Start clean.
- Water. Rainwater is ideal because there's no chlorine. If you're using tap water, let it sit uncovered for 24 hours so the chlorine off-gasses, or use a dechlorinator.
- A small aquarium air pump with airline tubing and an airstone. The kind for a fish tank works perfectly.
- Three to four scoops of good, finished compost. The quality of your compost is the most important variable in this whole process. Rich, dark, full of earthworms and fungal hyphae — your tea will be excellent. Half-finished or biologically thin compost gives you a thin tea.
- A food source for the microbes: one to two tablespoons of unsulfured molasses for bacteria-dominated tea, or kelp meal or humic acid for a fungal-dominated tea.
- A mesh bag or cheesecloth to strain the finished tea before application.

That's the whole setup. Nothing fancy.

## Step by Step

**Step one: Set up your air pump.** Run your airline tubing to the bottom of your five-gallon bucket with the airstone at the end. Turn it on before adding anything so you can confirm it's bubbling vigorously. You want good, strong aeration throughout the whole batch.

**Step two: Fill with water.** Fill your bucket about three-quarters full. If using tap water, let it sit the night before. Rainwater is always my first choice.

**Step three: Add your food source.** Put your molasses or kelp in now, before the compost. Stir it in. This gives the microbes an immediate food source as soon as the compost goes in.

**Step four: Add the compost.** Remove your earthworms first — they'll drown in the bucket. Add your three to four scoops of finished compost, then give it a gentle stir. You can put the compost in a mesh bag for a cleaner finished tea, or add it loose and strain at the end. I go loose — more surface area, more microbial contact with the water.

**Step five: Brew for 24 hours.** Keep the air pump running the entire time. You'll see foam forming on the surface as microbial activity picks up — that's a good sign. The liquid will darken and start to smell rich and earthy, like a forest floor after rain. That's what you want.

**Step six: Use it immediately.** Once you stop aerating, oxygen levels drop and aerobic microorganisms start dying off. You've got maybe two to four hours after turning off the pump before the tea degrades significantly. Apply it right away.

## How to Apply It

Two options: soil drench and foliar spray.

For a soil drench, strain your tea through cheesecloth or a fine mesh bag, then water it into the root zone of your plants. This is where you want it if your goal is building soil biology and improving nutrient availability. Apply in the morning or evening when soil temperatures aren't extreme.

For foliar application, strain it more finely — you don't want to clog a sprayer — then apply directly to the surface of your plant leaves. The microorganisms colonize the leaf surface and outcompete pathogenic fungi and bacteria. Some extension research supports this as a disease suppression strategy, though results vary depending on what pathogen you're dealing with.

I favor soil drenching for garden beds and containers. The root zone is where the microbial exchange actually happens, and that's where you want to concentrate your biology.

## The Soil Food Web Connection

Here's what I want y'all to really internalize about compost tea. It's not a fertilizer. It's not adding NPK numbers to your soil. It's adding biology.

The USDA NRCS Soil Biology Primer describes healthy soil as containing billions of bacteria, fungi, protozoa, and nematodes per teaspoon — each playing a specific role in the soil food web. Bacteria break down organic matter and fix nitrogen. Fungi extend their hyphae to move water and nutrients to plant roots. Protozoa graze on bacteria and release plant-available nitrogen as waste. Nematodes regulate bacterial and fungal populations. It's a whole ecosystem operating in miniature, and that ecosystem is what actually feeds your plants.

Gabe Brown talks about this constantly. The plant doesn't just absorb synthetic nutrients directly — it negotiates with the soil biology to get what it needs. The plant exudes sugars from its roots to feed specific microbial populations, which in return process and deliver specific nutrients. That relationship has been going on for 400 million years. The synthetic fertilizer pathway is 70 years old and bypasses all of that ancient biology.

Compost tea is a way of accelerating and delivering the ancient pathway. You're giving your soil more of the biology it needs to have those negotiations with your plants. Done consistently, on already-building living soil, it's one of the most powerful tools you have.

Use a clean bucket. Brew it 24 hours. Use it immediately. Start with the best compost you can get your hands on.

That's the whole thing.

---

## Sources

1. University of Arizona Cooperative Extension — Compost Tea 101: What Every Organic Gardener Should Know: https://extension.arizona.edu/sites/default/files/2024-08/az1739-2017.pdf
2. University of Connecticut Soil Nutrient Analysis Laboratory — Compost Tea: https://soiltesting.cahnr.uconn.edu/compost-tea/
3. Illinois Extension / University of Illinois — Compost Tea: Miracle Product or Snake Oil?: https://extension.illinois.edu/blogs/good-growing/2019-08-14-compost-tea-miracle-product-or-snake-oil
4. USDA NRCS — Soil Biology Primer: https://www.nrcs.usda.gov/resources/education-and-teaching-materials/soil-biology-primer
5. USDA NRCS — Healthy Soils Are Full of Life: https://www.nrcs.usda.gov/sites/default/files/2023-01/Healthy-Soils-Are-full-of-life.pdf
6. Ohio State University Extension — Understanding Soil Microbes and Nutrient Recycling: https://ohioline.osu.edu/factsheet/SAG-16

scottyQuotes: I started making compost tea because I read about it in a book.
Make sure to use a clean bucket. Bad microbes can mess things up.
Get about three or four scoops of good compost, take out the earthworms, bubble it up for 24 hours.
